title: Late Interaction Models
concept: ColBERT-style models that keep per-token vectors and interact at query time, sitting between bi- and cross-encoders.

exercises.prompt: In the two-stage tradeoff system we learned last time, how do we combine bi-encoders and cross-encoders?

options.text: We use cross-encoders to scan millions of documents, then bi-encoders to summarize them.
We use a fast bi-encoder for coarse retrieval, and a precise cross-encoder for final reranking.
We completely replace neural search with keyword matching to avoid latency.

exercises.explain: The two-stage retrieval pipeline uses a fast bi-encoder first to filter millions down to a few candidates, followed by a slow but highly accurate cross-encoder to rank the final results.

cards.title: The Best of Both Worlds?
cards.body: We know bi-encoders are lightning fast but lose detail, while cross-encoders are deeply accurate but painfully slow. What if we could build a hybrid that gives us token-level detail without the massive computation delay? Enter Late Interaction.

cards.title: What is Late Interaction?
cards.body: Late interaction models, like ColBERT, encode queries and documents separately (like bi-encoders) but keep individual vectors for every single word token. They delay the interaction step until the very end, comparing all query tokens against all document tokens using a fast alignment step.

cards.title: The Index Skim Analogy
cards.body: Imagine a bi-encoder as checking a library catalog and a cross-encoder as reading every book word-for-word. A late interaction model is like skimming the index of the top books: you don't read the whole text, but you still match specific terms to find the exact pages you need.

cards.title: Late Interaction in Action
cards.body: In a ColBERT search for 'bakery croissant recipe', the query tokens ('bakery', 'croissant', 'recipe') are matched individually against the token vectors of a document. The system finds the maximum similarity for each query word against all document words and sums them up.

cards.title: Not as Slow as You Think
cards.body: You might think late interaction is just as slow as a cross-encoder because it compares individual tokens. In reality, because the document token vectors are pre-computed and stored in advance, the query-time comparison is just a fast matrix multiplication.

cards.title: Comparing Interaction Timing
cards.body: Comparing how different models handle the interaction between query and document.

left.text: Early joint attention. Deep token-to-token interaction from the start. Very slow.

right.text: Separate token encoding first. Fast alignment of token vectors at the very end.

exercises.prompt: Where do late interaction models sit on the trade-off spectrum?

options.text: They sit between bi-encoders and cross-encoders in both speed and accuracy.
They discard all token-level information to save space.
They require passing the query and document together through a joint Transformer.

exercises.explain: Late interaction models stand between bi-encoders and cross-encoders by keeping separate encoders but allowing token-to-token comparison at the end.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In a late interaction model, document token embeddings can be pre-calculated and stored before a query is ever made.
exercises.explain: Documents can be encoded offline ahead of time because the query and document are still processed through separate neural networks.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe what late interaction models preserve for comparison:
exercises.sentence: Late interaction models preserve token-level details by keeping vectors for every word instead of compressing the text into one single vector.
exercises.explain: Unlike bi-encoders which compress everything into a single global vector, late interaction models preserve token-level details for richer matching.

exercises.prompt: Identify the description of a late interaction model.

options.text: A model that condenses the entire document text into a single 768-dimensional dense vector representation.
A model that outputs multiple vectors per document and uses a MaxSim operator to align query and document tokens late in the pipeline.
A model that concatenates the query and document at the input layer before feedforward processing.

exercises.explain: Option B is written by an AI as it uses technical jargon ('MaxSim operator', 'all-pairs token interaction') to describe the mechanic of late interaction models, whereas the others describe standard bi-encoders or cross-encoders.

exercises.prompt: How do ColBERT-style late interaction models balance high accuracy with fast query performance?

options.text: We pre-compute and store per-token embeddings for documents, then run a fast token-to-token similarity alignment at search time.
We feed the combined query and document tokens into a single Transformer model only at query time.
We compress documents into a single global vector and queries into another, then calculate a simple cosine similarity.
We bypass neural embeddings entirely and rely on classic keyword-counting algorithms like TF-IDF.

exercises.explain: Late interaction models like ColBERT compute and store token-level embeddings during document ingestion. At search time, the query's token embeddings are compared to these pre-computed document token embeddings using a fast MaxSim operator. This achieves near-cross-encoder quality with bi-encoder-like speeds because the heavy document encoding is done offline.
